name: memory-sequence-game (/memoria wiring)
prismaModelSnippet:
model MemorySequenceSession {
  id             String   @id @default(cuid())
  tenantId       String
  chatId         String
  telegramUserId BigInt
  seed           Int
  length         Int
  startedAt      DateTime @default(now())

  @@unique([chatId, telegramUserId])
  @@index([tenantId])
  @@map("memory_sequence_sessions")
}
botUpdateServiceIntegrationNotes:
Everything above targets apps/bot/src/bot-update.service.ts, which I did not edit myself (per instructions). Five places to touch, in order:

1. Imports — three existing import statements need one more name each, plus a fourth gets two more names (see part 1 of the snippet for exact anchors: which existing name to insert before/after, so alphabetical lint order is preserved). No new import statements are needed; `@superbot/data`, `./tokens.js` and `@superbot/module-games` are all already imported in this file.

2. Module-level constants/helpers (part 2) go where `RPS_EMOJI`/`parseTelegramUserIdArg`/`isGamificationMissionKind` already live, just before `@Injectable()` — same free-function style already used there, not class methods.

3. Constructor (part 3): this is the pattern used by every other `@Optional()`-injected repository with an InMemory fallback (see `coopMissionRepository`, `chatSettingRepository`, `guardianRepository` immediately above it). Append it as the very last constructor parameter.

4. botHandlers() (part 4): insert the two new entries between the existing "trivia.answer" and "quiz.scores" entries — it's the natural home next to the other free-standing minigames. Order matters here only in that "memory.answer" should not be placed ahead of anything that itself consumes plain-text messages unconditionally (nothing currently in this file does — chat-activity.logger only observes and returns null/undefined, it does not short-circuit dispatch).

5. Handler methods (part 5): insert right after `handleTriviaAnswer` ends, before `handleFilesCommand` begins.

Two OTHER files need a small change that I deliberately did NOT touch (out of scope per instructions), but are necessary for this to run for real (not just via the InMemory default):

- apps/bot/src/tokens.ts — add one line: `export const MEMORY_SEQUENCE_REPOSITORY = Symbol("MEMORY_SEQUENCE_REPOSITORY");` (same one-liner pattern as every other token in that file).

- apps/bot/src/app.module.ts — add one provider (same shape as the `COOP_MISSION_REPOSITORY`/`GUARDIAN_REPOSITORY` entries already there):
  { provide: MEMORY_SEQUENCE_REPOSITORY, useFactory: () => new PrismaMemorySequenceRepository() }
  (plus importing `MEMORY_SEQUENCE_REPOSITORY` from "./tokens.js" and `PrismaMemorySequenceRepository` from "@superbot/data" into that file's existing import lists.)

Design notes on behavior:
- No plaintext answer is ever stored — only `seed` + `length`; `generateMemorySequence` is re-run at verify time from those two values (mirrors modules/games/src/memory-game.ts, which was already implemented/tested and untouched by me).
- One open session per (chatId, telegramUserId) — starting /memoria again replaces any unfinished prior attempt (upsert on a unique (chatId, telegramUserId) key), so there is no way to get "stuck" with a stale session blocking new games.
- The answer window (30s) is enforced at verify time by comparing `Date.now()` against the stored `startedAt`, not via any in-process timer — correct for a stateless webhook-driven bot.
- Every attempt (right or wrong) clears the session — deliberately single-try, to avoid an indefinite window where any digit-containing chat message in that chat gets treated as a guess.
- Digits only (max value 9), so a reply can be "3 7 1 0", "3,7,1,0" or "3710" with no ambiguity — parsing just strips non-digits.
- Reward: `gameRepository.addScore(...)` with the sequence length as points (reuses the existing GameScore table/leaderboard already shared with trivia/quiz — no new score table).
- Nothing here weakens or removes any existing gate; this is purely additive (a new opt-in command plus a narrowly-scoped text-message consumer that only activates for a user who just started this specific game).

Verification performed: wrote packages/data/src/memory-sequence-repository.ts + its test; to typecheck the Prisma-backed class I temporarily appended the MemorySequenceSession model to packages/data/prisma/schema.prisma, ran `prisma generate`, ran `tsc -p packages/data/tsconfig.json --noEmit` (zero errors attributable to this file — the file produced no errors at all), ran the test file with vitest (7/7 passed), then removed the temporary schema addition and re-ran `prisma generate` to restore the client to its original (pre-existing, already-modified-by-another-task) state. `git diff --stat` on schema.prisma before/after matched exactly (9 insertions/3 deletions, unrelated Guardian-verification changes), confirming no residue was left. packages/data/src/index.ts got exactly one new export line appended at the end, matching the established "NEW: ... (file-name)" comment convention already used there by sibling wiring tasks.
risks: Low risk, fully additive: no existing command, gate, or handler is modified or weakened. The only behavioral surface risk is the free-text answer handler (handleMemoryAnswer) "stealing" a message from normal chat — but it only activates when (a) the sender has an open /memoria session in that exact chat AND (b) their message contains at least one digit, so it cannot affect any user who hasn't just run /memoria, and for such a user the blast radius is limited to 30 seconds. Two follow-up file edits (not made by me, per scope) are required before this is live end-to-end: tokens.ts needs the new Symbol, and app.module.ts needs the Prisma provider registration + import — until then the @Optional() default (InMemoryMemorySequenceRepository) will silently run without persistence (fine for testing on @Modryvatestbot, not for production). The prismaModelSnippet must be merged into schema.prisma and `prisma generate`/migrate run before the Prisma-backed path works at all; I verified this compiles cleanly by temporarily doing exactly that and then reverting, so no further typecheck surprises are expected once it lands for real. One minor UX tradeoff, intentionally chosen for simplicity per the task's instruction: replies are single-attempt (right or wrong closes the session) rather than allowing retries within the window.
